data.title: John Hancock Preferred Income ETF Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary Highlights

  • The U.S. bond market rose in the first quarter as bond yields declined sharply.
  • The John Hancock Preferred Income ETF fund (NYSEARCA:JHPI) underperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA U.S. All Capital Securities Index.
  • Security selection within the communications sector was a positive contributor to performance, while security selection within the electric utilities sector was a detractor.

Market review and outlook

U.S. bonds recorded positive returns in the first quarter. The bond market experienced a sell-off early on driven by strong economic data, including a better-than-expected jobs report in January. However, bond yields subsequently declined sharply as signs of slowing economic growth emerged, highlighted by a significant drop in retail sales and the lowest consumer sentiment reading since November 2022. Furthermore, changing U.S. trade and tariff policies created considerable uncertainty, resulting in risk-off market sentiment during the latter half of the quarter. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained short-term interest rates throughout the quarter while revising its economic projections downward and raising its inflation forecasts.

In this context, bond yields fell across the board, with intermediate-term bond yields decreasing the most. Sector performance mirrored the risk-off sentiment, with U.S. Treasury securities and government agency residential mortgage-backed securities delivering the best returns. In contrast, high-yield corporate bonds and asset-backed securities lagged for the quarter.

With the shift in market focus from the Fed and inflation to tariffs and implications for growth, interest rates rallied sharply and credit spreads weakened. We anticipate that the Fed will continue to depend on data and not react impulsively to the Trump administration's policy announcements. While broader economic data has softened somewhat, labor markets remain stable and inflation appears to be broadly contained, although still above the Fed's target.

Contributors and detractors

The fund underperformed the overall preferred securities market in the first quarter. Positive contributors to performance included security selection in the communications sector as well as the banking and insurance sectors.

The portfolio's detractors included security selection in the electric utilities and brokerage asset manager sectors. Furthermore, the portfolio's security selection in preferred stock was the top asset class detractor.

Portfolio positioning

We favor defensive sectors, such as electric utilities, which have demonstrated resilience during stressful periods and provided solid downside protection. This sector is primarily insulated from the current tariff war. Most of the preferred universe consists of financials, which are expected to experience little to no direct effect from tariffs, particularly in the insurance sector. There are still attractive opportunities to generate income within credit and spread sectors, with the potential for spread compression and limited risk of permanent capital impairment.

data.title: Hormel Foods: No Rush To Buy, Given Ambitious Guidance
data.description:
Shares of Hormel Foods (NYSE:HRL) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 10%. The company has struggled with rising input costs that have been difficult to pass on to customers without weakening volumes. Additionally, its marquee Planters brand has struggled to maintain relevance with younger consumers. While it has been a disappointing year for its investors, shares did rise 1% on Thursday despite reporting mixed Q2 results, suggesting the bad news may be factored into valuation. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if HRL is worth buying. There are tentative signs that fundamentals are turning, but not enough to add shares.

In the company’s fiscal second quarter, Hormel earned $0.35, which beat estimates by a penny, as revenue was flat year over year at $2.9 billion. EPS was down about 8% from last year. Organic sales growth was 1%, though volumes were down 7%. Hormel has been able to raise prices to increase revenue and recapture some cost, but clearly, consumers remain very price sensitive, leading to a nearly equally sized drop-off in volumes. This reflects a difficult operating environment with limited discretionary income. On the earnings call, management said the retail environment is “choppy” with a strained consumer, which, I believe, is a fair description of the environment.

Lower volumes reduced operating efficiencies, and as such, Hormel reported just a 16.7% gross margin. Even though sales rose by $11 million, gross profit fell by $19 million, reflecting some of these pressures. The company is closing a plant in California, moving that production to its two remaining plants, which should help improve unit costs. Given lower gross profit, operating income fell by $17 million to $248 million as margins compressed 50bps to 8.6%.

These pressures came even as there are some tangible benefits to management’s cost-cutting effort, the transformation and modernize (T&M) initiative. Management reiterated $100-$150 million of benefits from this program this year. Validating this claim, SG&A was $15 million lower at $251 million. One headwind for activity in the quarter was that HRL pulled back on advertising ahead of a large push in H2. Advertising spending was $36 million, down $8 million from last year. It expects advertising costs to rise double-digits in H2, largely to support new products from Planters, SPAM, and other key brands.

Looking at segment results, retail accounts for 60% of the business. Here, sales were flat despite a 7% drop in volume, thanks to price increases. Thanks to cost cuts from its T&M program, profits rose by 4%. Lower commodity shipments drove the sales decline. Importantly, Planters is showing sequential improvement (though still declining), and its Applegate brand reaccelerated to 6% sales growth. Its Mexican offerings are also growing, thanks to strength from Herdez.

Planters has been posting declines, but management expects sales growth to turn positive later this year, given its new marketing push. New product launches, including its nut “duo” packs, have helped to liven the brand, and an ongoing focus on protein-oriented and healthy snacking should be a favorable trend for the brand. Applegate has seen strong take-up of its breakfast offerings.

Foodservice volume was down 1%, excluding the impact of divestitures. Due to price increases, organic net sales rose by 4% to $936 million. Profits fell by 4%, given margin pressure, and it is seeing end-market softness. This unit is particularly exposed to traffic at convenience stores, and consumer spending on snacks here can be quite discretionary. Given the tepid consumer environment, I struggle to see a case for much acceleration here.

Finally, international volume rose 9%, but profits fell by 21%. It has seen solid exports and strong growth in China, in particular, where it operates locally. Brazil was a source of weakness. This unit is just about 7% of the business, and so the ultimate impact is negligible. The US is a net exporter of agriculture products, so insofar as trade tensions lead to countervailing duties, that could pose a risk. Importantly, it has a local presence within China, which insulates it from some of these trade pressures.

One key segment for the business is Turkey, where supply has been a source of pressure, which has weakened margins. It has taken time to pass increases on to consumers, though its shift towards value-add products has helped to insulate it somewhat. Management is seeing some sequential improvement in pricing and believes most costs will be recaptured during the key “whole bird” Thanksgiving selling season.

Alongside results, management tightened its guidance range. It now expects full year sales growth to be 2-3% and anticipates that tariffs will be just a 1 to 2 cent headwind. Interestingly, while it raised the bottom of its sales estimates, it lowered the top of earnings estimates, suggesting greater margin pressures than previously expected, partly due to tariffs.

Frankly, even with the top end lowered a bit, this is a fairly ambitious guide, with management clearly expecting growth to accelerate in H2. Its implied H2 guide assumes over 22% operating income growth from last year, or over $700 million. It is forecasting at least $0.88 in H2 earnings from $0.70 in H1. Management is quite clearly betting on the recent momentum in Planters and turkey pricing to accelerate, aided by a more aggressive marketing stance. Frankly, I think there is still some skepticism on the ability to deliver this level of acceleration, and otherwise, I expect shares would have reacted more positively. This skepticism is merited

It remains an open question whether HRL can deliver enough margin expansion to flex up operating income that much, especially with increased promotional activity. Indeed, you can see that producer prices (its costs) are now rising more quickly than consumer prices. You can see from the below how input prices began pressuring margins in 2022, then producers recouped this. But now, we are entering a more difficult margin environment.

Given the difficulty Hormel has had pushing higher input costs on to consumers without sacrificing volumes, I am concerned about the margin backdrop, especially given the need to see accelerating sales growth to meet its guidance targets. Given the summer driving season boosting c-store activity and seasonality around turkey, there should be H2 improvement relative to H1, but I see full year EPS as likely to be in the $1.53-$1.63 range, bracketing the low end of guidance.

Now, I would note that Hormel has a strong balance sheet, with $670 million of cash and $2.9 billion of debt. Especially as pre-summer inventory builds reverse, we should see strong free cash flow generation over the balance of the year, and with debt already at the low end of target, Hormel has capacity for bolt-on M&A or further dividend growth. Shares have a 3.9% dividend yield. Management is committed to dividend growth, with 59 years of increase. I expect that streak to continue

With $1.62 of forward earnings power, I see Hormel trading with an ~18.7x multiple. This is not a compelling entry point, especially for a stock that is flat over the past decade and down over a third in the past five years. Hormel’s guidance is aggressive, and I see downside risk, given rising input costs and questions about how quickly it can reaccelerate Planters' sales. Given its balance sheet and the likelihood of some improvement, I do not see a rush to sell shares, and ~$30 strikes me as fair value for a low-growth company with a solid balance sheet and secure dividend. Still, I would look elsewhere for upside and view shares as a “hold.”

data.title: Spotify: With Healthy Premium Adds And Margins, It's Time To Buy In (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Amid a turbulent macroeconomy, the phrase “controlling the controllable” has been common C-suite speak during the Q1 earnings season. And while it’s true that many companies are focusing more on profitability amid the prospect of slower growth, few companies are truly “walking the walk” and committing fully to expense reduction.

Spotify (NYSE:SPOT), however, is truly starting to showcase its profit muscle, beginning with deep layoffs that the company initiated in late 2023. Spotify’s expense base is actually falling year over year, while the company continues to enjoy double-digit growth in paid subscribers and ad revenue. To me, that’s a formula for a company that can be relied on to generate consistent earnings and cash flow growth.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Spotify in March, when the stock was still trading at around $540 per share. At the time, I had cheered the company’s successful price increases for driving reliable revenue and earnings growth, while also questioning the company’s valuation multiples. While valuation still remains a weakness for the stock, I think Spotify deserves due credit for its stiff expense reductions that have allowed for massive jumps in profitability, and I have heightened confidence in the company’s ability to grow into its multiples. I’m upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

Beyond the company’s recent burst in profitability, there are a number of reasons that investors have to be bullish on Spotify:

  • Despite being a household name, Spotify continues to see double-digit growth in total users as well as paid subscribers. This showcases that Spotify still has a greenfield market opportunity to cover as it enters new markets and expands its accessibility through a variety of subscription plans at different price points.
  • Price increases have driven healthy revenue growth and proven the stickiness of Spotify’s base. After successive rounds of price increases in major markets (the standard Individual plan now runs $11.99/month), the company hasn’t seen any major changes in churn.
  • Other price increases in the subscription space are normalizing the field for Spotify. Netflix, for example, also continues to push up prices - training consumer behavior to potentially anticipate further price hikes for streamers like Spotify.
  • Playlists and diversified content also drive retention. Spotify has a reputation for maintaining great playlists, and migration to rival platforms like Apple Music is often a challenge for longtime users because of their saved music and playlists - allowing Spotify to stand out even if its individual subscription pricing sits slightly higher than peers.

Of course, where we have to be mindful of Spotify is its valuation. There are no direct peers that Spotify has in music streaming, but when we compare Spotify to its closest peer Netflix (NFLX), we find the company trades at higher adjusted EBITDA multiples.

That being said, Spotify is still in the early innings of generating economies of scale on its growing subscriber base. Its operating margin is still more than doubling y/y, indicating that its FCF and earnings are still in the early innings and that Spotify still has plenty of room to grow into its multiples.

Amid consistent performance and great user addition trends, I’m comfortable paying a modest premium for this stock in exchange for its high quality. Stay long here and keep riding the recent upside higher.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Spotify’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Spotify's revenue grew 15% y/y to €4.19 billion, barely decelerating at all from Q4's 16% y/y growth pace. And as shown in the chart below, the company hit 678 million total monthly active users (MAUs) in the quarter, while adding 5 million net-new Premium subscribers to end at 268 million paid members, up 12% y/y. The company exceeded its own expectations for Premium adds by 3 million: usually, Q1 tends to be a softer quarter for Premium adds as the company pulls in a lot of marketing around its Wrapped campaign in Q4.

The strong beat in Premium adds versus Spotify's own expectations is a telling signal that Spotify is maintaining its grip as a countercyclical company: despite consumer spending weakening across the board, Spotify is still able to drive healthy retention and net-new signups. The company's guidance also calls for adding yet another 5 million net-new Premium subscribers in Q2.

The company notes that its subscriber beats are coming primarily from emerging markets (this is a risk we have to call out: developing markets users tend to come in at a lower ARPU). The company has executed targeted campaigns in order to drive market share growth in emerging markets. Per co-president Alex Norstrom's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

This was our highest Q1 subs net add since 2020 and our second highest Q1 ever. And a huge part of this boost came from emerging markets.

These markets drove two thirds of the subs outperformance, with places like Latin America and Asia Pacific coming in especially strong. But it's not just the emerging markets that are doing well. Developed markets are also seeing solid growth [...]

Our promotions can be highly localized and targeted. They're geared at converting new and long-time free users that have seen the exceptional value that Spotify Premium really provides. The bottom line we have a number of different tools available to us to continue to drive healthy subscriber growth. And you saw some of those at play in Q1."

The company has also noted strong business development in its ad-supported plans. In Q1, the company noted that advertisers have responded very well to new automated ad-buy features that Spotify has rolled out. This led to active advertisers growing sequentially from Q4 to Q1, something the company says has never happened before.

Buoyed by economies of scale as content costs per head come down, Spotify achieved a 403bps y/y expansion in gross margin, with Premium gross margins increasing 332bps y/y and ad-supported margins more than doubling (+885bps y/y) to 15.3%. The company notes in particular that stronger-than-expected ad sales on podcasts helped to lift ad-supported gross margins meaningfully higher.

Spotify combined natural economies of scale with a tight lid on operating costs. The chart below showcases the company's trailing twelve month operating costs in the green vertical bars: on a TTM basis, we note that Spotify's expense base has shrunk by -2% y/y.

Many tech companies are touting the benefits of AI in eliminating mindless work and enabling headcount reduction, but few are taking it to the level of actually committing to expense reductions. This is one major reason that Spotify is standing out against other tech stocks: alongside healthy gross margin gains, opex controls have helped the company triple its pro forma operating income to €503 million, or a 12.1% margin that improved 750bps y/y.

Key takeaways

Few consumer brands are as iconic and beloved as Spotify. In a tough macroeconomic environment, Spotify has shown its resilience by showcasing minimal churn and better-than-expected user adds despite its multiple rounds of price increases. At the same time, Spotify has taken an axe to its own expense base, leading its operating margins to more than double y/y.

Despite the stock's premium, Spotify appears to have found the right formula to balance growth and profitability, and its deeper penetration into emerging markets ensures that it has a healthy growth trajectory ahead. Stay long here.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 Equity Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1 Highlights

● In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February.

•Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.

● The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2. 78% in January before falling in February (-1. 30%) and March (-5. 63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U. S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2. 80% in February from a year earlier-higher than the Fed's 2. 00%target-as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U. S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned-4. 27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6. 94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany (+16. 00%), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2. 93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3. 00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U. S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U. S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2. 78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U. S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio (MUTF:JHEAX) recorded a slight loss for the quarter but outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 70% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index and a 30%weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net). An out-of-benchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) notably contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U. S. stocks also added value in a period during which U. S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) detracted, mainly due to stock selection in information technology, communication services, and financials.

data.title: CEF Insights: Income Potential In Multi-Sector Bond Strategies
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Mike Buchanan, Chief Investment Officer with Western Asset Management. Western Asset Management is the investment advisor for Western Asset Diversified Income Fund, symbol, NYSE:WDI.

Mike, we're happy to have you with us today.

Mike Buchanan:

Really happy to be here, and thanks for having me.

CEFA:

Mike, I mentioned the Western Asset Diversified Income Fund, an actively managed strategy that invests in a broad range of public and private fixed income securities. Can you discuss the investment strategy as well as the key objectives of the fund?

Mike Buchanan:

Sure. The mission of WDI is to look for higher levels of reliable, predictable, consistent streams of income across all areas of fixed income. We do have a secondary goal of capital appreciation, but really think of it as an income-oriented portfolio. We leverage Western Asset’s expertise and a whole host of different areas. So, think corporate credit, and that means investment grade credit, high-yield, loans. That's both U.S. and non-U.S. Structured Credit, which includes residential mortgage securities, commercial mortgage securities and more ... everything from hotel lodging, to industrial, retail office as well as probably to a more limited extent asset-backed securities. And then finally, emerging markets in all forms. And the way we think of emerging markets, that's emerging market corporate securities, it's dollar-denominated sovereign securities, it's local currencies that are sovereign. So really looking across all those areas and making dynamic allocations across those sectors as we see relative value opportunities. And one thing, I think it's an important thing to note, is that all these sectors, although they're somewhat correlated, don't move in tandem. They have periods where one's outperforming another, the prospects for one are either more encouraging or more bleak. And so we really do try to be dynamic and use the idea of sector rotation to add value for our investors.

CEFA:

With regard to the exposure the strategy provides to private credit markets, are there unique characteristics to this area of credit and does private credit provide a degree of diversification to a portfolio that investors may not get from more traditional fixed income investments?

Mike Buchanan:

So the whole topic of private credit is really interesting, and we all probably hear about this disintermediation, what I call the lines are blurring between public liquid credit and private credit. And there isn't a rigid line between those two, meaning something's not distinctly just public or it's not distinctly just private credit. More and more, we're seeing things that are somewhere in that border territory. And what's great about WDI is it can really take advantage of some of those, what I would call more private-like investments. And the tradeoff, really, I think as everyone knows, is that when you buy a private security, you're giving up some liquidity. And what we've found is the advantage we get for giving up some of that liquidity is we're able to negotiate a better structure that could be like call protection that could include better collateral claims, so including more collateral in the overall loan that we're making, as well as just an overall better yield, better pricing.

So WDI, again, because of the closed-end nature, can take advantage of some of those less liquid opportunities and some of the more private credit areas that we invest in. And that includes both corporate as well as structured credit. So yes, WDI is definitely taking advantage of that for investors. I think managers that can look at the whole spectrum ... everything from private and pure private [credit] all the way to very liquid public [credit] and make those relative value calls and can make those assessments about when it's appropriate to take less liquidity in exchange for a better structure, more yield, more return—those are going to be the managers, the investors who have a competitive advantage going forward.

CEFA:

Mike, we mentioned that this strategy invests in a broad range of securities. What is your process to evaluate this investment universe to develop a workable set of potential investments?

Mike Buchanan:

It's a really good point. I mean, it's a big universe of securities that we start with. I say it starts with resourcing. If you're going to manage a portfolio like WDI well, you have to have adequate resourcing, and that means you have to have made the investment in specific teams that have an expertise in each one of these areas that we invest in. So that's really the starting point. And at Western Asset, we certainly have made an investment in all those areas that I mentioned earlier that span from corporate credit all the way to emerging credit and different forms of structured credit. We have deep teams with a distinct focus on fundamental research. That's very, very important to us.

To get more to the question, each team has a qualitative and a quantitative process that they use to home in on specific opportunities within their investible universe that they feel have a favorable risk-reward skew. And then from there, what we really do is we have what, I think, is a really great process for looking at and comparing and contrasting relative value across the different sectors. We're able to look at investment grade credit and compare it with what we're seeing in non-agency mortgages and perhaps compare that to what we're seeing in emerging market corporate bonds. So we've been doing this for a while, having those relative value discussions, and it really does give us the insight and the perspective that we need to make those dynamic allocation decisions that I mentioned earlier.

CEFA:

How do you then make specific security selections and allocate those positions as you build your portfolio?

Mike Buchanan:

Another really good question. I'm the lead portfolio manager on WDI, but I work with other portfolio managers as well. We work with each team to really understand and try to home in on the specific investment that we're talking about and then consider if it is consistent with the goals and the objectives of WDI? Over time, our portfolio managers in these sectors have these conversations, they really have an appreciation and an understanding for what those goals and objectives are for WDI. What's unique about those? Again, I would say just as one example that focus on income is really, really important. Not as much of a focus on total return, but just on consistent, reliable income. They understand that we could sacrifice a little liquidity if we think we're getting a better overall credit, we're getting more collateral claim, and we're getting better structure. So it's a very customized approach. Again, we work specifically with each team to talk about all the securities that are potentially candidates for investment within WDI, and then we really go through the more difficult process of selection as well as the sizing of those investments.

CEFA:

What factors or events would lead you to sell a particular portfolio security or significantly change your portfolio allocation?

Mike Buchanan:

Let me start with that second question. In terms of what would cause us to change our portfolio allocation? Again, as I mentioned, this is a dynamic strategy. We are constantly looking at all the sectors that we can invest in, and we're comparing and contrasting fundamentals, technical conditions, overall valuations, and really making that difficult relative value call. Where are the best opportunities for our investors in WDI? And we take that very seriously, and we do take advantage of it. The process that guides us in that is a committee, we call it our Global Asset Allocation Committee. That's a committee where I'm one of the co-chairs. And that really gives us the framework for discussion again, where we look at the things that I mentioned, fundamentals, technicals, relative value. And we have the team leaders really going through and debating and discussing the merits of the particular sectors. And ultimately, these are the insights that we use to guide our allocation decisions.

In terms of the first question you asked, what I would call ... a little bit on our sell discipline. When do we sell a security out of WDI? There are few conditions that would necessitate a sale of a security in WDI. The first would simply be if fundamentally we got something wrong. And that's something that we do a lot of disciplined, fundamental work on: the securities that we put in the portfolio. And I'd like to say that a hundred percent of the time we're going to be right. But we constantly have to go back, evaluate how these credits are evolving, how the outlook may or may not have changed and make ongoing decisions. And sometimes when we get it wrong, and you start to see that a credit is moving in the wrong direction, you see evidence of deterioration or impairment, and you're starting to get concerned that ultimately this is going to be a security that won't be able to deliver on that reliable consistent stream of income. We take immediate action on those, and we sell those securities assuming that they are above what our estimated recovery value is in the event of a default.

So that's the first way a security is sold—if basically we got it wrong, or it's going in the wrong direction, we think there's risk of impairment not delivering on what we ultimately set out to do when we invested in that security. Also, I would say a security becomes a candidate for sale when it reaches or exceeds its relative value target, and that's determined by the analyst who follows that investment as well as the portfolio manager. At the time of investment, they make relative value targets. And when a security hits that target, it becomes a candidate for sale. This doesn't mean we automatically sell it, we look for other securities that can give us a superior risk/reward, a superior level of income and return. And sometimes that takes a while. So it doesn't mean when it hits its relative value target, it immediately is sold, but it's certainly a candidate for sale. And we're always looking for ways that we can enhance the overall income characteristics of WDI, and we have a pool of securities usually that are in that relative value target area that we can choose from. So there's a lot of work that goes into selling securities, and we're very cognizant of the primary focus of income on WDI and making sure that we can keep those consistent reliable streams.

CEFA:

Mike, the Federal Reserve has held rates steady, the rate of inflation has improved, and while the US economic growth has been resilient, the policies of the new administration have introduced some volatility to investment markets. Where do you see the fixed income markets currently, and what is your outlook for the rest of 2025?

Mike Buchanan:

You said the key word there, volatility. Coming into 2025, we expected a year where you were going to see elevated uncertainty, elevated volatility, and a lot of that was simply the administration's policies on everything from trade and tariffs, to immigration, to taxes, to deregulation. There were a lot of unknowns and a lot of ambitious policy initiatives that may or may not ultimately get to the finish line. And we knew that would come with an element of uncertainty. So it's not really surprising us that we're seeing so much volatility and so much uncertainty so far this year. But we do ... and you alluded to this. We ultimately see inflation going lower. It's like you'll see a temporary uptick that comes from tariffs, but that's not going to be ongoing. And that ultimately you will see inflation slowly but surely making progress toward the Fed's target of 2%, and we think that'll give the Fed room to lower their policy rate.

Now does that mean that the rest of the curve is going to go lower with that policy rate? That's probably a little more debatable there. We do think we're likely to see the curve steepen from current levels, meaning that the shorter rates are going to move more quickly lower than longer rates. But, ultimately, we think that unlike two of the last three years, rates are not going to hurt you in 2025 and are perhaps more likely to help you; be a little bit of a tailwind. So that's a good starting point. Just having a backdrop where rates are either stable to going lower, you combine that with high yields that we have now and that's a byproduct of some pretty difficult years in fixed income with yields going higher. It has reset valuations. So in all those areas that I talked about, yields are relatively high. And what's nice about that is you always have to not just look at yields, you have to look at the overall fundamental backdrop. And right now, our view is the fundamental backdrop is actually pretty solid. Corporations are in good shape from a balance sheet standpoint, earnings standpoint, margins, and so you move over to other areas that we invest in. Again, it's a pretty solid, sound fundamental backdrop.

So that means that you're going to enjoy that high yield; that's a starting point. You're probably going to give up only a little due to impairment because of a good strong fundamental backdrop, and you're obviously earning a lot of income on the way. So when you put it all together, our view is and has been that 2025 is ultimately going to be a good year for fixed income, and WDI is certainly in a good place from a structural standpoint to take advantage of that.

CEFA:

What are the most significant risks you consider in the current environment?

Mike Buchanan:

It's really interesting where we are right now. So it's a recession. I would say any credit-oriented portfolio, that's always going to be one of your biggest risks, is that you get your base case wrong. And again, our base case is more of a soft growth, but still positive growth scenario with inflation trending in the right direction and fundamentals holding up well. But, if we were to slip into a recession, obviously that starts to put more pressure on fundamentals. It usually translates into spread widening. And what could possibly cause that recession? Because again, I mentioned we think we're at a pretty good starting point when it comes to the economy, when it comes to corporations, even when it comes to the consumer. We're at a decent starting point in terms of where we are now. But obviously, tariffs have become front and center and, as we're speaking today, it's unclear if we are going to start to get some trade deals.

And it's really unclear—are these tariffs or are these tariff negotiations designed simply to just achieve better trade outcomes of better trade deals with our key trading partners, which I think the markets would be okay with. Or is it something more than that? Are we really trying to rebuild the industrial base in the United States? Are we really trying to bring manufacturing back to the US? And I think if the administration really digs in on that, that's a much more difficult thing to do. It's a much more difficult thing to see to fruition. And it obviously brings a lot of uncertainty from our trading partners. I think depending on which direction these tariff negotiations go, that could be the difference between our base case and then something more scary that could lead to a recession. Again, if the administration really digs in and wants to make this about rebuilding the industrial base and bringing all manufacturing back to the US, that's just going to take time. There's no real certainty about that and probably brings with it a whole host of different challenges.

CEFA:

How is the WDI portfolio currently positioned, and how has this changed over the past few months?

Mike Buchanan:

So one thing that we've been doing recently ... I mentioned that volatility that we've seen in the market and the spread widening that we saw. In particular, at the beginning of April. So, recently, we were able to take advantage of that and use some of the borrowing capacity that we have to go after attractively priced securities at wider valuations. If you look at the portfolio at the beginning of the year versus where we are now, we have been gradually adding a little bit to our high-yield exposure. It's been a little more focused on high quality, high yield. Again, you won't get as much income with higher quality high yield, but you're buying securities that have the ability to endure economic challenges. So if our base case is wrong, if we slip into a recession of some sorts, I think the kinds of securities that we're buying, or at least adding to the portfolio, are more durable. They can hold up better in that kind of environment. So we've been carefully adding to some of the higher quality high yield. We've added a little bit to our loan book as well. But, again, using new cash that we've drawn on our borrowing facility to accomplish that.

Another thing that we've been doing pretty much over the last couple quarters is moving to a little bit of a higher quality bias. So looking for those swap opportunities that will allow us to go, for instance, from a single B rated security to a double B rated security or go from an unsecured security to a secured security. It's really an acknowledgement of trying to get a little more defensive, understanding that the outlook, the economic outlook, is just a little more cloudy. It's not as certain, and as much as we have conviction in our base case, we know that other cases around that, including a recessionary case, aren't an impossibility. There is a probability. So we want to make sure that we can muscle through those types of environments and really have minimal harm or impairment to the income streams in our portfolio.

CEFA:

Where are you seeing the best opportunities to put new capital to work?

Mike Buchanan:

I guess the way I would think of that question ... I will comment on one particular area, but it's more about patience. Because, again, at the beginning of the year we said this is going to be a year where there's going to be some volatility, there's a lot of uncertainty, and what we want to do is be in a position to take advantage of that volatility. So we are being more tactical, like I said on this recent spread widening, we added some exposure in different areas. I think if the markets were to perform well over the next few months and spreads tighten, I don't think we would be shy about selling into that a little bit because, again, I think with volatility it means you're just going to have opportunities to add securities in the portfolio at more attractive levels. So we're really exercising patience; we're trying to be disciplined about that. We're trying to be disciplined about when is it the appropriate time to perhaps sell a little bit? When is it an appropriate time to take advantage of volatility, to buy at wider spreads?

And in terms of what we target, again, the market's going to give us some guidance on that. I would say one area that we're watching pretty closely, and it is closely correlated with rates. But if we were to start to see a more reliable trend lower with respect to interest rates, and not just policy rates, but a little further out the curve, the five year, the 10 year, if our conviction was higher on that part of the rate environment moving lower, you could definitely see us moving a little more aggressively into some of the commercial mortgage securities that are out there in the market. We've got a really good team that looks at that and is constantly doing analysis there.

And, like I said earlier, they look at all different sectors within commercial mortgages, and they do think that fundamentally that sector has been turning for the positive and we're starting to see more new issuance. Unlike some of the other areas in the market, valuations still look relatively attractive when compared to historical averages. So there's definitely some opportunity there, but it's very correlated with the direction of rates, and we want to have a stronger view on that. So I would say that's just one area where we're very involved in terms of keeping our eye on what's going on there, identifying some potential securities that we'd want to move on, but we just need a little more confidence in terms of the direction of interest rates.

CEFA:

Mike, how do you see an allocation to a diverse, actively managed fixed income strategy like WDI best positioned in an income-oriented investor's portfolio?

Mike Buchanan:

I think WDI is a really powerful strategy for income oriented investors. The current distribution rate in that 12 to 13% range is really attractive, and I think if you look historically, look at our dividend, we've had a good track record of either stability or being able to increase that dividend. I think that can just serve as a key component of an income-driven investor's portfolio because of that high, consistent income. And it really complements other, perhaps more growth oriented securities, that might be in a portfolio. Especially in tax advantaged accounts. When you look at the current environment and the uncertain outlook—we talked about tariffs, geopolitical uncertainty, volatility—we believe that a well-diversified bond portfolio with broad exposure to income producing asset classes is just a valuable addition to almost any investor's portfolio. And, again, given the uncertainty, it just makes a lot of sense in our view to have an income strategy playing a role in your portfolio today. The other thing I would say is maybe not recently, but over the past few years, equities have performed well, and we think it's prudent that if you have a strategy that provides compelling yield as a diversifier, especially as investors look to navigate through the market challenges ahead, WDI is a really good candidate for that.

CEFA:

Mike, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Mike Buchanan:

Well, thank you. I really appreciate the opportunity.

CEFA:

And we want to thank you for tuning into another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: Diversification Redefined: Exploring The S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index
data.description:
By Wenli Bill Hao

The S&P 500® continues to be a trusted benchmark for the broad U.S. market, measuring the performance of U.S. large-cap companies across various sectors. As the market moves, certain stocks and sectors grow to represent a larger share of the index, highlighting their significant influence on the economy. While this is the natural result of market-cap weighting, some market participants are exploring alternative strategies in pursuit of enhanced diversification.

In this blog, we introduce the S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index, which aims to mitigate concentration risk and address sector imbalances by reweighting companies within The 500™. This innovative approach employs a hierarchical equal sector weight methodology, utilizing Syntax’s FIS® sector taxonomy.

FIS’s Sector Taxonomy

This six-level classification system captures the diverse business models and product lines of companies. As shown in Exhibit 1, the FIS sector taxonomy consists of 8 sectors, 24 sub-sectors, 72 industries, 193 sub-industries, 311 business activities and over 3000 individual product lines in the S&P 500 universe.

Index Methodology

The S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index equally weights each of the eight primary sectors defined by Syntax FIS, with quarterly rebalancing.1 Within each sector, level 2 sub-sectors are equally weighted, as are level 3 industries, and so forth down to business activities level (see Exhibit 2). At the lowest level, within each business activity,2 stocks are weighted proportionally based on the revenue generated within that specific activity.

Diversification Beyond Primary Business

Unlike traditional classification systems that categorize companies solely by their primary business segment, the FIS classification scheme enables a single company to be represented across multiple sectors and industries. As a result, if a company generates revenue from more than one business activity, its final weight in the index is the sum of the weights from each of its business activities.

As shown in Exhibit 3, over 41% of S&P 500 companies engage in business activities across more than one FIS sectors, collectively accounting for 62% of the index’s total weight.

A Short- and Long-Term View of Performance

Exhibit 4 illustrates that the S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index has historically outperformed the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index in terms of both absolute returns and risk-adjusted returns, across both short- and long-term periods.

Characteristics of the S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index

Sector Level Diversification

Exhibit 5 illustrates that the S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index boasts the smallest sector weight spreads and a higher effective number of sectors3 compared to its counterparts.

Stock Level Diversification

Although primarily designed to equalize sector weights and balance business risks, the S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index has also historically reduced single-stock concentration. Compared to The 500, it maintained a higher effective number of stocks and featured lower maximum stock weights (see Exhibit 6).

Conclusion

The S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index has had a more balanced representation of sectors and business risks within the S&P 500 universe. Furthermore, it has historically had lower single-stock concentration. Moreover, the S&P 500 Diversified Sector Weight Index has historically outperformed the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index.

1 Please refer to S&P 500 Diversified Sector Index methodology for more detailed info.

2 Within each Business Activity, each company is assigned a weight based on the product of (1) the weight of that Business Activity and (2) the trailing last 12 months’ revenue that the company derives from that Business Activity, divided by the sum of revenue that all companies derive from that Business Activity. If a company derives revenue from multiple Business Activities, a company’s final weight is the sum of the weights from each of the company’s Business Activities.

3 Effective number of sectors is defined as the inverse of the sum of squared sector weights for each index.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Total returns may reflect a waiver of part of the Fund’s fees for certain periods since inception, without which returns would have been lower. Indexes are unmanaged and provided for comparison purposes only. No fees or expenses are reflected. You cannot invest directly in an index.

In terms of individual stock detractors, FPT, the Vietnamese information technology services business, underperformed along with the Vietnamese market after higher-than-expected proposed tariffs from the US Administration. Gamma Communications (OTCPK:GAMCF) weakened along with other mid-sized companies despite posting strong results. The company is shifting from the AIM to the main market, which may cause some short-term capital flows. Poly Medicure, the producer of consumable medical devices for the Indian and European markets, underperformed along with the wider Indian stock exchange.

Among the main positive contributors to performance, CTS Eventim (OTCPK:CEVMF)(OTCPK:CEVMY), the European market leader in ticketing for live entertainment, performed well during the quarter as its results confirmed the resilience of the business and its leadership in Europe. Dino Polska (OTCPK:DNOPF)(OTCPK:DNOPY), the Polish food retailer, had a positive quarter after management commentary illustrated confidence that margins in fiscal year 2025 should continue to expand relative to fiscal year 2024. GTT, the French specialist in liquefied natural gas transport technology, rose on strong results, which led to a rebound in the shares after previous weakness caused by the restructuring of the company’s green hydrogen unit and the CEO’s resignation.

Market review

Global equity markets ended lower over the quarter. Investors were initially optimistic about stronger economic growth after US President Trump’s inauguration. However, as the quarter progressed, concerns grew over the economic impact of his tariff policies amid fears of a global trade war.

European equities rose over the period. German Chancellor-designate Friedrich Merz announced plans to significantly increase defence spending and launch a €500 billion infrastructure fund over the coming years, enabled by relaxing the country’s constitutional debt brake. Eurozone GDP grew by a higher-than-expected 0.2% sequentially in the third quarter, having expanded by 0.4% in the previous three months. Annual consumer price inflation fell from 2.3% in February to 2.2% in March, which was lower than expected but just above the ECB’s 2% target. The ECB cut the deposit facility rate by 25 basis points (bps) at both its February and March meetings, taking it to 2.50%.

UK equities ended higher over the period. Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced £14 billion of spending cuts in the Spring Statement to help improve the UK’s fiscal position. The UK economy unexpectedly expanded by 0.1% sequentially in the fourth quarter after stalling in the prior three months. Meanwhile, annual inflation fell from 3.0% in January to a lower-than-expected 2.8% in February but stayed above the BoE’s target. The BoE lowered its Bank Rate by 25 bps to 4.50% in February.

Japanese equities declined after annualised GDP growth came in lower than expected at 2.2% in the fourth quarter, while inflation remained above target, prompting the Bank of Japan to raise its key short-term interest rate by 25 bps to 0.50% at its January meeting. Emerging markets rose, supported by a weaker US dollar and stronger Chinese equities on stimulus hopes.

Activity

We made two new initiations and sold one holding during the quarter.

Sweco is the leading architecture and engineering consultancy company based in Sweden and has diversified to become a business with international reach across a number of Western European markets. Sweco specialises in sustainable buildings, efficient infrastructure and clean water access. In total, the company works on around 120,000 projects annually, so it is not overly exposed to any single contract. Management targets top-line growth of 12% per annum (compared with a consensus of around 6%).

Elite Material Corporation (EMC) is a Taiwanese producer of copper clad laminate (CCL), a key component used in printed circuit boards. Looking ahead, EMC should benefit from its substantial exposure to high-end CCL, where the total addressable market is expected to grow at a compound rate of over 28% per annum over the next three years. M8 – the highest-end CCL – has an average selling price around two and a half times higher than the previous-generation M7 and is approximately five times more expensive than the industry average. Therefore, as demand for high-end CCL increases, EMC should experience meaningful revenue growth and operating leverage.

Meanwhile, we sold our holding in Mani, the Japanese producer of medical syringes, after disappointing results that highlighted its continued challenges in the Chinese medical market.

Beyond this, we made trades to reduce the Fund’s market sensitivity and its exposure to companies that could be significantly affected by US tariffs.

Outlook

Macroeconomic volatility remains elevated, as US trade policy continues to evolve. Negotiations are still underway. However, the policy carries implications for US inflation, monetary policy and, more broadly, for global growth. While the exact outcome of these developments is difficult to predict, uncertainty itself often weighs on consumer and business activity and could lead to an earnings slowdown. As such, markets — particularly those with a high degree of ownership concentration — may be vulnerable to further correction.

Given the uncertainty, we believe it is critical for investors to focus on ‘quality’ companies — those with the financial strength and adaptability to flex their supply chains, a deep understanding of customer needs and the market-share clout to maintain pricing power.

A final point to note is that the small-cap asset class may hold up better than its large-cap counterpart. Small caps tend to be more domestically focused and therefore less exposed to tariff uncertainty. Moreover, valuations for small-cap companies are well below those of large caps, and small caps have been less widely held, meaning any shareholder unwind could be less severe.

data.title: Seritage Growth Properties: Ongoing Costs Eat Into Its Net Asset Value
data.description:
Seritage Growth Properties (NYSE:SRG) was down to 16 properties at the end of Q1 2025, consisting of around 1.6 million square feet of gross leasable area and 240 acres of land. This included nine wholly owned properties with around 0.8 million square feet of gross leasable area and 132 acres of land.

The pace of Seritage's property sales have slowed significantly though (it sold one asset in Q1 2025), and it is facing a July 2026 term loan maturity date (assuming it pays a fee to extend the maturity from July 2025).

I have modeled a scenario where Seritage pays off its term loan by July 2026 and ends up finishing its sales process (and winding down its business) by the end of 2026.

That scenario results in an estimated value of $2.80 per share for Seritage's common shares if it receives around the balance sheet values for its remaining properties. I am thus neutral on Seritage at its current share price.

Property Valuations

Seritage currently expects approximately $600 million to $785 million in gross proceeds from the sales of its remaining properties. This is based on its asset sales under contract or in purchase and sale agreement negotiations, plus Seritage's future sales projections.

Seritage's future sales projections have generally trended down over time due to the challenging market environment.

Thus for the purposes of this report, I am assuming that Seritage will net $500 million in proceeds from the sale of its remaining assets.

This is close to the $514 million balance sheet value of those assets at the end of Q1 2025, calculated by adding up Seritage's net investment in real estate, investment in unconsolidated entities and real estate held for sale.

Cash Position And Ongoing Expenditures

Seritage had $107 million in cash (including restricted cash) at the end of Q1 2025. Adding $500 million in net proceeds would bring this up to $607 million.

In a scenario where it winds down its business (and sells all its assets) by the end of 2026, it may end up spending around $139 million in cash from Q2 2025 to the end of 2026.

I've assumed that Seritage's revenue basically offsets its property operating expenses and real estate taxes.

I've also estimated that Seritage spends $70 million on cash G&A during the seven quarters between now and the end of 2026. This includes severance costs and other costs associated with winding down the business.

There is also $40 million allocated for ongoing development costs. The run rate for this would go down as Seritage sells off properties.

$ Million
Cash G&A $70
Cash Interest $20
Preferred Dividends $9
Development Costs $40
Total Outlay $139

This would result in Seritage having $228 million in cash at the end of 2026 after paying off the $240 million remaining on its term loan.

The term loan currently matures in July 2025, but Seritage can pay 2% of the outstanding term loan balance (this payment is included in my cash interest assumption) to extend the term loan maturity until July 2026.

Notes On Valuation

Seritage's receivables and prepaid expenses are currently roughly the same as its payables, so I'm assuming that's a wash. This would mean that the value left for Seritage's common stock would be $158 million after the $70 million in Series A preferred shares are redeemed.

This is approximately $2.80 per common share, which is right around Seritage's current share price. I am thus neutral on Seritage at the moment.

Seritage's Q1 2025 book value (net of the $70 million in preferred shares) is approximately $5.50 per common share. However, the various ongoing costs basically wipe out that margin of safety.

Other Scenarios

An investment in Seritage's common shares is thus basically a bet that it can net more than $500 million for its remaining properties, while wrapping up all its asset sales in a timely manner.

A scenario where Seritage nets $550 million for its properties and winds up operations by the end of 2026 would leave its common shares with approximately $3.70 per share in value instead.

However, if Seritage winds up its business by the end of 2027 instead, it may incur another $40 million in costs. This assumes it has paid off its term loan by July 2026 and also has somewhat reduced G&A and development costs in that final year.

Thus if it winds up its business by the end of 2027 and nets $550 million for its properties, Seritage's common shares would be worth close to $3 each.

However, if it winds up its business by the end of 2027 and nets $500 million for its properties, its common shares would be worth approximately $2.10 each.

Conclusion

Seritage appears to have a net asset value that is significantly higher than its current share price. However, various ongoing expenditures such as G&A, interest and preferred dividend payments and development costs need to be taken into consideration.

After factoring those items in, I estimate Seritage's value at $2.80 per common share in a scenario where it nets 97% of balance sheet value for its properties and winds up its business by the end of 2026.

I am thus neutral on Seritage at the moment. Potential upside would come from netting a higher amount for its properties and/or winding up its business sooner than the end of 2026.

data.title: Snap-On: Margins Will Keep Expanding As Vehicles Become More Complex
data.description:
Snap-on (NYSE:SNA) is definitely not a sexy stock, and you won't frequently see it on the news, but the company is as reliable as a Swiss watch. Since 1936, it has been paying quarterly cash dividends without a single decrease or pause.

If you go to a mechanic's workshop, there is a high chance you will see Snap-on's tools, and probably, you will get good comments about them from the mechanic.

And this is the reason the company caught my attention in the first place. Despite what seems an easy-to-replicate but mission-critical product, mechanics keep buying from Snap-on.

Investment Thesis

Despite operating in a mature market growing at mid-single digits, Snap-on benefits from the all-time high average age of vehicles, requiring more frequent repairs, and increasing complexity.

As vehicles include more electronic components, Snap-on's revenues from diagnostic tools and software (the highest margin segment) increase, as we saw during Q1 2025.

Snap-on will grow EPS at ~8% per year, and taking into account the 2.8% dividend yield, I expect Snap-on to deliver a 10-11% rate of return going forward.

Snap-on's Business Model

Snap-on makes money by selling tools, equipment, and diagnostic systems used to repair and maintain vehicles and infrastructure in any industry involving nuts and bolts.

The company has a market cap of $16.8B, generated $5.1B in revenues during 2024, and a free cash flow of $1.1B (expected to decline by 7% in 2025).

The Snap-on Tools Group consists of revenues generated through its 4,700 franchised van network to repair shops and mechanics.

Snap-on's franchises purchase tools from the company at a discount and have pre-established routes where they visit repair shops. Also, Snap-on receives monthly fees, although this is not a meaningful source of revenue ($19.4M in 2024 compared to almost $2B from tools sold through this channel).

The Repair Systems & Information Group's (RS&I) revenues are generated directly through Snap-on's distribution channels, and it has higher operating margins (25.7% in Q1 2025), since it doesn't have to share the profits with franchises.

The Commercial and Industrial Group has lower margins (15.5% in Q1 2025), and it serves industrial companies in the aerospace, natural resources, military, transportation, and agriculture sectors.

Finally, the Financial Services division (~70% operating margins) generates interest income by financing big-ticket purchases.

Competitive Advantage

Many investors might think of Snap-on as a manufacturer of spanners and screwdrivers, and while it is true that these are some of its products, some others are significantly more complex.

Diagnostic systems generated about 20% of Snap-on's revenues last year, and these are way stickier than screwdrivers.

Snap-on has a wide range of diagnostics tools depending on the industry, usage, and customer needs, and once a repair shop gets used to a particular product, it is unlikely it will change to a competitor.

One of Snap-on's main competitive advantages is its strong reputation and long track record of quality products.

Even with products that are easier to replicate, on many occasions, customers will prioritize buying a well-established brand over a new, less-known product.

Imagine, for example, a vehicle lift. This is a one-time spending for a repair shop (or one every 50 years), since these are long-lasting tools.

However, I don't consider Snap-on a highly defensive business, given that in an economic slowdown, some repair shops may shut down or look for a cheaper alternative.

Albeit Snap-on can charge a premium for its superior quality, brand reputation, and wide range of tools (one-stop shop), its pricing power is limited.

Solid Balance Sheet

Snap-on has a rock-solid balance sheet with zero long-term debt and $1.3B in cash.

While the company doesn't need so much cash to operate the business, and it could be returned to shareholders through a special dividend or buybacks (the stock is not trading at unreasonable levels), I believe they might be awaiting some opportunistic acquisition.

Its assets also include $0.8B in receivables and $1B in inventories. One aspect that I don't view positively is the decrease in inventory turnover, although the company has also increased its receivables payment period and managed to keep returns on capital at the same levels (~17%).

Growth Driven by Margin Expansion

To grow, Snap-on doesn't need to reinvest substantial amounts of capital in infrastructure due to its franchise model, and D&A expenses are less than 10% of its FCF.

Although it has a negative working capital and needs to increase its inventories as it grows, it benefits from operating leverage and the increasing complexity of vehicles (more on that later).

Gross margins remained flat (~51%) during the last decade, but operating margins before financial services expanded from 16.3% a decade ago to 22.7% in 2024.

Despite the modest 4% revenue growth since 2006, earnings per share have grown at a 14% CAGR due to margin expansion.

Since the repair and maintenance tools market is mature and volume growth opportunities are limited, Snap-on deploys some of its free cash flow into acquisitions to expand the business.

Capital Allocation Strategy

Snap-on has a clear capital allocation strategy. The first priority is reinvesting in the business and then closing acquisitions of complementary businesses that share the company's values.

One positive thing about Snap-on's acquisition strategy is that the risk of making an impactful mistake is limited.

The acquisitions are relatively small, and they do not experiment with new segments. During the last decade, they deployed $580M in acquisitions, which is ~8% of the net income generated during the same period.

We're not looking to transform our business. We're looking to find businesses that do what we do, and we know dramatic -- we know clearly who we are.

We are a company who doesn't make their money by the penny, makes their money in the critical where we can get a premium for solving these problems.

If we see somebody who operates in the critical, we'll consider acquiring them. But we won't go into retail, we won't venture into things like retail.

Source: Nicholas Pinchuk (CEO). Bank of America Industrials, Transportation & Airlines Key Leaders Conference May 2025.

The third and fourth priorities are dividends and buybacks. At the moment, Snap-on pays a 2.8% dividend yield and has reduced the share count by 0.7% during the last twelve months.

Tailwinds: Aging Vehicles and Increasing Complexity

Last year, the average vehicle age in the U.S. was 12.6 years, and it increased even further to 12.8 years according to an S&P Global report published a few days ago.

While many industries get negatively affected by tariffs, macro uncertainty, and inflation, Snap-on and the aftermarket industry benefit from the decline in new vehicle purchases.

Also, as vehicles include more electronic components and become more complex, fixing them requires more diagnostic tools. This not only increases revenues but is also positive from the margins perspective, since it is the highest margin segment of the business.

As the car gets more complex, the software that we have that's proprietary becomes more essential to do the repair. That's why you're seeing software goes up in RS&I.

Souce: Nicholas Pinchuk (CEO). Oppenheimer 20th Annual Industrial Growth Conference May 2025.

Expected Growth

During Q1, the only segment that grew was the RS&I (3.7% organic revenue growth), and margins improved by 140bp, driven by higher sales of diagnostic tools.

The franchise and industrial segments' revenues declined by 6.8% and 2.9%, respectively, driving Snap-on's overall margins down to 21.9% from 22.9% a year ago, and reducing EPS by 8% YoY.

The main reason for the decline has been the macro uncertainty and lower spending from repair shops due to the negative sentiment.

Following the miss on earnings during the first quarter, the full-year estimates have been revised downwards, and the EPS expectations for 2025 have been reduced from $20.17 to $18.7 (2.6% yearly decline).

Even with the recovery in consumer sentiment, I expect a slightly higher decline in EPS (-4% to -6%) compared to the analyst consensus.

Although the degree of uncertainty will be lower compared to the days following "Liberation Day" and the first tariff announcement, I don't see Snap-on's clients rushing to purchase tools that are relatively expensive.

However, it is important to take into account that unless there is a strong economic recession, the delay in tool purchases will recover in the upcoming quarters.

From a long-term perspective, I see no reason to forecast higher than historical revenue growth rates, which is in line with independent research forecasts ranging from 3.5% to 5.2% CAGR.

Assuming a 4% revenue growth, a 50bp operating margin expansion from operating leverage and faster growth from the higher margin RS&I segment, and a 0.7% decrease in share count per year, Snap-on will grow EPS at ~8% per year.

Considering the 2.8% dividend yield, I expect Snap-on to deliver a 10-11% rate of return going forward.

Valuation

Even though the expected rate of return of Snap-on's business might not be attractive enough at the moment, I consider it a quality business that could become a compelling investment if bought at a lower multiple.

If Snap-on keeps missing expectations in the upcoming quarters, the valuation could decline to the 11-12x EPS multiple range.

In that situation, the 10-11% expected return could become more attractive if we add the chance of a multiple expansion.

For now, I view Snap-on as a hold.

Risks

Snap-on has a solid balance sheet and does not pursue risky acquisitions. Overall, I do not see high disruption risks for the business model, and as long as it can keep delivering superior products at a fair price, its reputation will remain intact.

The major risks I foresee are higher raw materials prices (steel) and a tough macroeconomic environment.

A substantial percentage of its tools are made of steel, so the company's margins are sensitive to its price, as was proven in 2022 with a 130bp decline in gross margins.

The positive aspect is that the company has 10 manufacturing facilities in the U.S., so the impact of tariffs is reduced substantially, as it only needs to import some materials, but not the final product.

The other major risk is a hard recession. Although Snap-on's business is not highly cyclical, since repair shops and the rest of the industries it serves will need its mission-critical tools to keep operating, they can delay purchases in an uncertain environment.

This can be clearly seen by looking at its performance during the great financial crisis. In 2009, revenues declined by 17.2% and net income by 43%, and it took three years to recover 2008's revenues.

Bottom Line

All in all, Snap-on has an above-average business model.

The company has a strong balance sheet, attractive returns on capital, and some competitive advantages (albeit with limited pricing power).

While I expect margins to keep expanding as the average vehicle age increases along with its complexity, driving higher diagnosis tools, it operates in a mature market with low revenue growth opportunities.

At the current price, Snap-on is a hold until it decreases to a more attractive valuation ($250 per share would make it a buy).

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 Plan - Portfolio 2041-2044 Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1 Highlights

  • In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February.
  • Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, especially related to trade and tariffs.
  • The Fed kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024.

Market review and outlook

In the first quarter of 2025, the bellwether S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) recorded a mid-single-digit decline after posting yet another fresh all-time high in mid-February. Postelection optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs, and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 2.78% in January before falling in February (-1.30%) and March (-5.63%). It was particularly dragged down by growth stocks and small-cap stocks.

During the quarter, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) kept its key overnight lending rate unchanged following three rate cuts in the final four months of 2024. The central bank indicated that further reductions would depend on real progress on inflation or unexpected weakness in labor. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.80% in February from a year earlier—higher than the Fed's 2.00% target—as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Against this backdrop, the S&P 500 Index returned -4.27% in the first quarter. However, international developed-market stocks gained 6.94%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index, buoyed partly by strength in Germany (+16.00%), where February brought elections and optimism that the new administration led by Friedrich Merz would pursue a pro-growth agenda. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 2.93% advance. Some emerging European markets, including Poland (+31%), Greece (+23%), and the Czech Republic (+29%), posted strong returns, bolstered by an improved outlook for the eurozone following Germany's plans to boost spending on defense and infrastructure. China (+15%) was also a standout performer; however, Taiwan (-13%) and India (-3.00%) were relatively weak.

Investment-grade bonds rebounded during the quarter as U.S. Treasury yields eased. Long-term Treasuries and Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities did particularly well amid growing economic growth and inflation concerns. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

Almost every measure of consumer and business sentiment, or soft data, has notably deteriorated amid rising uncertainty around trade policy. While sentiment usually lags hard data, such as employment and spending, today's soft data signals a far more dire outlook than the hard data implies. The real concern is whether businesses and consumers will continue to face this level of uncertainty for a prolonged period. With trade disputes lasting for nearly a year and a half during President Trump's first term, the sentiment could be right this time around, foreshadowing a deeper economic slowdown. Given the heightened risk, we continued to lower U.S. equity exposure while adding to European equities on an upside potential related to Germany's increased fiscal spending on defense and infrastructure and a more unified commitment from leaders across the eurozone on improving competitiveness.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a slight loss for the quarter but outpaced its blended benchmark, comprising a 70% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index and a 30% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net). An out-of-benchmark stake in the Real Assets Fund (T. Rowe Price) notably contributed for the quarter, as this asset class outperformed core equities, driven by the precious metals and energy groups. Exposure to non-U.S. stocks also added value in a period during which U.S. equities lagged. International Growth Fund (John Hancock) was a key relative contributor, aided by security selection in the industrials sector. Equity Income Fund (T. Rowe Price) also provided a relative performance boost, largely on the strength of security selection within industrials and consumer staples. On the other hand, Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) detracted, mainly due to stock selection in information technology, communication services, and financials.
